summary: Spolsky argues that UI consistency across programs is one of the most important principles of good software design, yet it is constantly undermined by designers' desire to be creative and programmers' anti-Microsoft sentiment. He traces the history from DOS-era chaos (where every program had different commands) through the Mac revolution (where consistent UIs let users learn many programs) to the Windows era, using Microsoft's acquisition of FrontPage as a case study in how consistency creates favorable impressions. He criticizes Netscape 6.0 and other programs that reimplemented standard controls for the sake of creativity, noting that inconsistency breaks accessibility features and confuses users. The core message is that designers should copy established conventions from dominant programs rather than reinventing interfaces, channeling their creativity elsewhere.
key_points: Consistency directly causes ease of use: when FrontPage copied the Office UI, Microsoft's program manager found it immediately intuitive, leading to a $150 million acquisition.

The pre-GUI era demonstrated the cost of inconsistency — users had to memorize basic commands like 'exit' for every single program, and Mac users adopted far more programs than DOS users because of consistent UI conventions.

Reimplementing standard platform controls (as Netscape 6.0 did) creates unforeseen compatibility problems, breaking accessibility tools, language input methods, and expected behaviors like right-click context menus.
Anti-Microsoft ideology and creative ambition are the two forces that fight against consistency, but both ultimately hurt users who have already internalized existing conventions.
Being inconsistent is almost always more work than following platform conventions — it requires writing and maintaining complex workarounds to override default behavior.
money_quotes: Consistency causes ease of use which in turn causes good feelings resulting in more money for you.

If consistency is so obviously beneficial, why am I wasting your time and mine evangelizing it? Unhappily, there is a dark force out there that fights against consistency, and that is the natural tendency of designers and programmers to be creative.
Being inconsistent is almost always more work than just acting like your platform expects you to act.

If you refuse to do it on some general religious principle that Bill Gates is the evil smurf arch-nemesis Gargamel, then you are just gratuitously ruining your program so that you can feel smug and self-satisfied, and your users will not thank you for it.

Microsoft spends more money on usability testing than you do, they keep detailed statistics based on millions of tech support phone calls, and there's a darn good chance that they did it that way because more people can figure out how to use it that way.
The only benefit to having funny symbols instead of 'OK' and 'Cancel' like everyone else is that you get to show off how creative you are.

The real quote is 'A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds.' Good UI designers use consistency intelligently, and, though it may not show off their creativity as well, in the long run it makes users happier.
key_insight: UI designers should suppress their creative instincts and copy established conventions from dominant programs, because users' mental models are shaped by the software they already know, and violating those expectations always costs more than it gains.
